title: Local Current Algebra for the HK Universality Class
abstract: We show that a Hamiltonian in terms of the local real-space currents obeying an $\mathfrak{su}_1(2)$ affine Lie algebra eliminates the non-locality in the Hatsugai-Kohmoto model for a doped Mott insulator. We establish this local correspondence through the Bjorken-Johnson-Low prescription for anomalous commutators. With this result, we show that the charge susceptibility computed from the current Hamiltonian is identical to that with the elemental Fermionic fields. Consequently, the HK model is local in real space, though not in terms of the Fermionic fields, thereby eliminating the key criticism of this model and reinforcing the utility of current algebras for strong interactions.
summary: This paper resolves the non-locality criticism of the Hatsugai-Kohmoto model by showing it can be reformulated as a local theory using real-space currents. By establishing an su(2) affine Lie algebra structure, the authors prove that the physical response of the model remains unchanged, reinforcing the utility of current algebras for studying strong interactions.
main_problem: The paper addresses the criticism that the Hatsugai-Kohmoto (HK) model is non-local in real space by seeking a local, effective Hamiltonian in terms of real-space currents.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that a Hamiltonian based on an su(2) affine Lie algebra eliminates the non-locality of the HK model and yields a charge susceptibility identical to the original fermionic field model.
method: The study employs the Bjorken-Johnson-Low (BJL) prescription to evaluate anomalous commutators and uses a current algebra construction (Sugawara-like) to derive the effective theory.
model_or_system: The system is a 1D doped Mott insulator described by the Hatsugai-Kohmoto (HK) model, which captures Mott physics via the breaking of Z2 symmetry and is considered a solvable alternative to the Hubbard model.
key_observables: Charge susceptibility (direct and mixed components), charge density, and current-current correlators.
important_parameters: Interaction strength U, Fermi velocity vF, Fermi momentum kF, and the Dirac limit (large kF and mass m).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the Dirac limit, a quadratic dispersion, and focuses on the low-energy, long-wavelength (IR) dynamics of the hole-doped ground state.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the HK model and its non-locality problem, introduces the current algebra construction using the BJL prescription, derives the commutation relations and effective Hamiltonian, and concludes by verifying the consistency of the charge susceptibility.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
